# AI Agents for Law Firms in Austin, TX: 2026 Implementation Guide
> URL: https://manuv.co/resources/ai-agents-implementation-legal-austin-tx
> Published: 2026-06-10
> Vertical: Law Firms & Legal Practices | Location: Austin, TX

**TL;DR:** Austin is the fastest-growing legal market in Texas - the Austin-Round Rock MSA now holds roughly 15% of the state's 113,771+ active attorneys, and venture funding into Austin startups hit an all-time high in 2025. But most small and mid-size Austin firms are losing intake to faster-responding competitors, burning associate hours on contract review, and operating under Texas Opinion 705's new AI duties without a formal deployment. The fix in 2026 is a three-agent Claude stack - intake, conflict-check, and document review - deployed in 90 days under Opinion 705 supervision rules. This guide covers exactly how to deploy it, what it costs, and what ROI to expect.

## Quick Facts
- Texas had **113,771 active State Bar members** as of 2023, and the **Austin-Round Rock MSA accounts for roughly 15%** of the state's active attorneys - making Austin the second-densest legal market in Texas after Houston-Dallas (State Bar of Texas, 2024 demographic report).
- **Texas Opinion 705 (February 2025)** establishes four binding duties for Texas attorneys using generative AI: competence, confidentiality, verification, and fair billing. Consumer ChatGPT and Claude.ai are presumptively insufficient for confidential client work under Rule 1.05.
- Average Texas attorney hourly rate is **$366** in 2025, with Austin commanding a **30-32% premium** over rural Texas markets. Associate rates at Austin firms run **$300-$625/hr** at boutique and mid-size shops (Clio Legal Trends 2026, TELA Fee Yearbook).
- **71% of solo firms and 80%+ of legal professionals overall** report using AI in 2025, but Clio's Legal Trends data shows small firms are *falling behind* mid-size firms in formal adoption - most use unmanaged consumer AI, which triggers Opinion 705 confidentiality risk.
- Lead response time under 5 minutes increases legal-intake conversion by **21x** versus a 30-minute delay (Lead Response Management Study / Harvard Business Review). The industry average response time is **42 hours**.
- **32.5% of Austin residents are Hispanic** and **18.6% are foreign-born** - the city projects Hispanic population growth from 40.6% to 47.1% of the metro by mid-century, making Spanish-language intake a non-optional capability for PI, family, and immigration firms (U.S. Census, Austin Monitor 2025).
- Austin venture funding hit an **all-time high in 2025**, with the largest single deal a $1B Series C for Base Power - meaning corporate, M&A, and emerging-companies practices in Austin have more transactional volume than any non-California secondary market.

## Why Austin law firms need AI agents in 2026

Austin is no longer a secondary legal market. The Austin-Round Rock MSA now holds roughly **15% of Texas's active attorneys** - more than 17,000 lawyers concentrated in a metro of just under one million people - and the practice mix has shifted hard toward technology, venture capital, life sciences, energy, and private equity. Kirkland & Ellis, Gunderson Dettmer, Latham & Watkins, Sidley Austin, and Pillsbury have all stood up substantive Austin offices since 2021. Gunderson's Austin office alone closed over 150 financings raising more than $4 billion in capital between 2021 and 2025.

This matters because Austin small and mid-size firms now compete against (a) BigLaw with infinite tooling budgets, (b) Bay Area transplants who arrive expecting Silicon Valley response times, and (c) more than 300 corporate relocations to Texas chasing legal services. Three forces are converging in 2026:

**First, client expectations are now Bay Area expectations.** A founder who texts a corporate firm at 10:30pm after a board meeting expects an answer that night. If your firm responds the next morning at 9am, the prospect has already called Wilson Sonsini or Gunderson. The Lead Response Management data shows that legal leads who get a response within 5 minutes are **21x more likely to book a consultation** than leads who hear back after 30 minutes. Austin's tech-adjacent client base has the lowest patience for delay of any U.S. market.

**Second, Texas Opinion 705 (February 2025) makes AI competence a binding duty.** The Professional Ethics Committee for the State Bar of Texas issued Opinion 705 in response to TRAIL (the Taskforce for Responsible AI in the Law), and it establishes four obligations: competence under Rule 1.01, confidentiality under Rule 1.05, verification of AI outputs, and fair billing. A firm using consumer ChatGPT for client work is now in default violation of all four. The fix is not to avoid AI - it is to deploy it on the commercial API with documented supervision.

**Third, the billing math no longer works.** Austin associates bill **$300-$625/hr** at boutiques and mid-size firms. Asking a second-year associate to spend three hours summarizing a deposition transcript is a $1,200-$1,800 task that Claude can do in eight minutes for under a dollar of API cost. Austin firms that re-allocate that time to higher-leverage strategy work are gaining share - the ones that don't are watching corporate clients migrate to Wilson Sonsini Austin, Cooley Austin, or one of the Sand Hill imports.

At manuv.co we see this every week with Austin clients: the question is no longer *whether* to deploy AI, it is *which three agents* to deploy first and how to do it without tripping a single Disciplinary Rule of Professional Conduct.

## The three-agent stack for Austin firms

Most Austin firms over-think this. They start by chasing a single all-in-one legal AI platform, get overwhelmed by the vendor landscape (Harvey, Spellbook, Clearbrief, Lexis+ AI, Westlaw Precision, Smokeball AI...), and end up with nothing deployed. The pattern that actually works in Austin deployments is a three-agent stack, each addressing a different bottleneck:

**Agent 1 - The bilingual intake agent.** Captures and qualifies new leads 24/7 across web chat, after-hours phone, and SMS - in English or Spanish based on the lead's first message. Books consults on the firm calendar, runs a conflict pre-check, and writes a structured note into Clio, MyCase, Smokeball, or Filevine. This is the single highest-ROI deployment for almost every Austin firm.

**Agent 2 - The Claude Code conflict-check skill.** A reusable script that runs against the firm's matter database to clear new conflicts in seconds instead of days, with an audit trail satisfying Opinion 705's competence and verification duties.

**Agent 3 - The document review agent.** Summarizes deposition transcripts, redlines contracts (high-volume task at Austin corporate firms), and surfaces issue spots from discovery production. Replaces 60-80% of the manual review hours associates currently bill.

Each agent is small enough to deploy in 2-4 weeks. Together, they cover the three places Austin firms leak the most value: leads that don't get answered (especially Spanish-speaking ones in East Austin and the I-35 corridor), matters that take days to open, and associate hours burned on review work that doesn't move the strategic needle.

## Agent 1: Claude-powered intake (bilingual)

The intake agent runs on three channels - web chat, after-hours phone (via Vapi or Bland), and inbound SMS. It is built on Claude Sonnet 4.5 with a system prompt that encodes the firm's practice areas, Texas-specific conflict-disqualification language, consultation booking rules, and bilingual fallback.

A realistic Austin personal-injury intake conversation looks like this: a prospective client texts at 11pm in Spanish after a rear-end collision on MoPac. The agent detects Spanish on the first message, acknowledges within 90 seconds in Spanish, asks for the basics (date, location, injuries, whether they sought treatment at Dell Seton or Ascension Seton), runs an instant conflict pre-check by pasting the other-driver's name into the firm's matter database, books a consultation slot from the firm's Calendly, and writes a structured intake note in English into Clio Grow before the intake partner wakes up. By 8am, the partner sees a qualified, conflict-cleared lead with a scheduled call - while three competing PI firms are still leaving voicemails.

### Why bilingual is non-optional in Austin

Austin is **32.5% Hispanic**, and the Hispanic share is projected to grow from 40.6% to 47.1% of the metro by mid-century. **18.6% of Austin residents are foreign-born.** For PI, family, immigration, and increasingly criminal defense practices, an English-only intake leaves money on the floor every single night. Claude is natively multilingual and handles Spanish (including regional Mexican Spanish, which is the dominant variant in Austin's Hispanic community since over 73% of the Hispanic population is of Mexican origin) at near-fluent quality. We configure the agent to detect language on the first message and respond in kind, with the structured case note always written in English for the firm's matter management.

### What makes an Austin intake agent compliant under Opinion 705

- All client communications are routed through Anthropic's commercial API (Claude Sonnet 4.5 or Claude Opus 4.5), with a Data Processing Addendum executed. Anthropic does not train on commercial API data by default.
- The agent's system prompt explicitly restricts it to triage and scheduling, never to legal advice - satisfying the verification duty under Opinion 705.
- Confidentiality language is included in the first message, satisfying Rule 1.05 duty to prospective clients.
- Conflict pre-check runs before any substantive case discussion.
- Transcripts are stored in the firm's matter management system, not in third-party chat logs.
- A designated supervisory attorney reviews transcripts on a documented cadence - satisfying the Opinion 705 competence and verification duties.

Deployment time at a typical 6-attorney Austin firm: 2 weeks. Monthly cost (Claude API, hosting, SMS gateway, optional voice gateway): around $450-$800. Replaces roughly $3,500-$5,000/month of after-hours answering service or paralegal-monitored chat.

## Agent 2: Claude Code conflict-check skill

Conflict checks are the unsung bottleneck of Austin mid-size firms - and especially of Austin corporate firms taking on emerging-companies work, where the related-entity graph (founders, prior portfolio companies, lead investors, board members) is enormous. Every new matter requires running adverse-party and related-entity searches against the firm's full historical client list, and at most Austin firms the process is still manual.

Claude Code skills are the perfect deployment unit for this. A Claude Code skill is a packaged automation that runs locally (often on a paralegal's workstation or a firm server), has scoped access to the firm's data, returns structured output, and can be versioned and audited - all qualities Opinion 705's verification duty effectively requires.

### What the skill does

1. Takes new-matter inputs (client name, opposing parties, related entities, witness list, and in corporate work the cap table and board roster).
2. Runs fuzzy-match queries against the firm's full matter database (Clio, MyCase, NetDocuments, Smokeball, Filevine, or SharePoint).
3. Flags exact matches, near matches, and waived prior conflicts.
4. Produces a markdown conflict-check memo that a partner can review and sign within 5 minutes.
5. Logs the run in the firm's audit log with timestamp, user, and inputs - satisfying the Opinion 705 verification and competence record-keeping expectations.

### Why this matters in Austin specifically

Austin corporate firms doing venture deals (Wilson Sonsini, Cooley, Gunderson, Latham, Sidley, Pillsbury all have Austin offices, but the same applies to local boutiques like DLA Piper Austin, Foley Gardere, and any 5-25 attorney emerging-companies shop) run more cross-entity conflict checks per matter than litigation firms do. A single Series A closing might require running conflict checks against the company, the founders personally, the lead investor, syndicate members, and prior portfolio companies of the lead - dozens of entities per deal. A manual check against a 30,000-matter Austin database takes a paralegal 30-90 minutes per matter; the Claude Code skill returns the same result in under 60 seconds with better recall (the fuzzy matching catches name variants and related-entity overlaps a paralegal would miss). For firms doing 15-40 new matter openings per month, this skill alone returns 15-40 paralegal hours per month.

## Agent 3: Document review and discovery summarization

The third agent is where the cost savings get serious. Austin firms - especially litigation boutiques along West 6th, commercial firms in the Frost Bank Tower, and the corporate shops downtown - spend enormous associate hours on document review, deposition summarization, discovery production analysis, and contract redlining.

A Claude-powered review agent does four things very well in the Austin context:

- **Deposition summarization.** Takes a transcript and produces a structured summary with witness statements indexed by topic, contradictions flagged, and key admissions extracted. Replaces 2-4 hours of associate time per deposition. At Austin associate rates of $300-$625/hr, that is $600-$2,500 of recovered time per deposition.
- **Contract redlining at venture-deal volume.** Compares an investor's term sheet or SPA against the firm's preferred terms (or NVCA model docs) and produces a clean redline plus a memo flagging deviations. Replaces 1-2 hours of associate time per contract.
- **Discovery review.** Processes large document productions in Travis County, Williamson County, or federal Western District of Texas matters, tags privileged and responsive material with confidence scores, and creates a privilege log draft. Replaces hundreds of associate hours per litigation matter.
- **Court filing deadline tracker.** Calculates dates per Travis County Civil District Court Local Rules (amended November 15, 2024) and federal W.D. Tex. local rules - the agent reads the local rule PDF, applies it to the matter's procedural posture, and surfaces a calendar of deadlines for partner review.

### The accuracy question

The legitimate concern is hallucinations - Claude making up facts not in the document. Texas Opinion 705's verification duty effectively requires solving this. The fix is technical and well-established by 2026: the agent runs in a retrieval-augmented configuration where every output is grounded in cited source passages, and the final review pass is a human associate spot-checking the citations. In practice, this still cuts review hours by 60-80% while improving consistency. The associate's job shifts from reading every page to validating Claude's pulled citations - a much higher-leverage task, and one that is fully compliant with Opinion 705's instruction that attorneys cannot blindly rely on AI outputs.

## Texas Opinion 705 and compliance posture

Every Austin firm we work with asks the same question first: what are the ethics risks? Here is the current Texas picture in 2026.

### Texas Opinion 705 (February 2025) - the foundational guidance

Issued by the Professional Ethics Committee for the State Bar of Texas at the request of TRAIL, Opinion 705 establishes four binding duties for Texas attorneys using generative AI:

- **Competence (Rule 1.01).** Attorneys must understand how generative AI functions and possess or acquire the skill to use these tools effectively and ethically. Blind reliance is prohibited.
- **Client confidentiality (Rule 1.05).** Attorneys must not disclose confidential information through AI inputs. Tools that inadvertently share information with third parties are unsuitable for client work.
- **Verification of results.** Attorneys must independently verify any information generated by AI before relying on it in client representation or court filings. Failure to verify can violate the duty of candor.
- **Fair billing.** Attorneys cannot bill for hours not genuinely worked, even when AI streamlines tasks. AI subscription and usage fees may be passed through to clients with prior agreement.

### Practical implications

- Consumer Claude.ai and ChatGPT are presumptively insufficient for confidential Austin client work - their consumer-tier business models include training and retention terms inappropriate for Rule 1.05.
- Anthropic's commercial API is acceptable when paired with a Data Processing Addendum and (where PHI is in scope, e.g. plaintiff-side med-mal) a Business Associate Agreement. Anthropic does not train on commercial API inputs by default, and a BAA is available under the sales-assisted Enterprise path.
- The Texas attorney remains personally responsible for AI-assisted work product. AI is a tool, not a co-counsel.

### Rule 5.03 (responsibilities regarding non-lawyer assistants)

Though Opinion 705 does not explicitly invoke Rule 5.03, the Texas State Bar's AI Toolkit and follow-on TRAIL guidance increasingly treat AI deployments as supervised non-lawyer assistance. A firm partner should be designated as the AI supervisor with documented review cadence - the same structural pattern New York firms use under their Rule 5.3.

### Bilingual disclosure

Texas does not yet have a synthetic-performer disclosure law like New York's GBL § 396-b. However, for AI voice agents answering Austin firm phones, best practice is to disclose AI identity at the start of the call - this satisfies both consumer expectation and pre-empts any future Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act exposure.

### Practical compliance posture

The three-agent stack above is fully deployable under current Texas rules when (a) the agents run on Anthropic's commercial API with a DPA in place, (b) the intake agent identifies itself as an AI assistant, (c) a designated partner supervises the deployment with a documented review cadence, and (d) every client-facing output is verified by a licensed Texas attorney before being relied upon.

## The 90-day deployment plan

Most Austin firms want a phased rollout that proves ROI before committing to the full stack. Here is the 90-day plan we use:

### Days 1-15: Discovery and intake agent

- Week 1: workflow mapping. We sit with the firm's intake coordinator, an associate, and the managing partner. We map every step from first lead touch to matter opening, identifying Spanish-language volume and after-hours leakage.
- Week 2: intake agent deployed to web chat only. Soft launch with monitoring - every transcript pushed to a Slack channel for partner review.

### Days 16-30: Intake expansion + conflict-check skill

- Week 3: intake agent expanded to after-hours SMS and phone (Vapi or Bland gateway with Claude Sonnet 4.5).
- Week 4: Claude Code conflict-check skill deployed against the firm's matter database. Two paralegals trained on the skill's output format and verification expectations under Opinion 705.

### Days 31-60: Document review pilot

- Weeks 5-6: document review agent deployed on one active litigation matter (Travis County or W.D. Tex.). Associates use it on deposition summaries and discovery review.
- Weeks 7-8: expand to contract redlining for the corporate practice group - typically venture transactions and commercial agreements.

### Days 61-90: Optimization and measurement

- Weeks 9-10: tune system prompts based on real transcripts. Most Austin firms find their intake agent needs a more conservative qualification standard after 30 days of conversations - the agent over-qualifies in week one.
- Weeks 11-12: run the ROI report. Compare consultation booking rate, average matter-open time, and associate hours per matter against the pre-deployment baseline.

At the end of 90 days, the firm has a real, working, Opinion 705-compliant AI agent stack and 90 days of measurement data showing exactly what it returned. The supervisory partner has a documented review log satisfying the Texas competence and verification duties.

## Real ROI benchmarks for Austin firms

Numbers vary by practice area, but here is what we typically see at Austin firms in the 4-20 attorney range after 90 days of the full stack:

- **Intake**: 28-42% increase in consultation booking rate. Most of the gain comes from after-hours coverage and faster speed-to-lead. Spanish-language intake alone typically adds 8-15% to qualified consultations for PI, family, and immigration firms.
- **Conflict checks**: matter-open time drops from 2-3 days to under 4 hours. Corporate firms doing venture deals see the largest gain (cross-entity conflict graphs).
- **Document review**: 60-80% reduction in associate hours per matter on review-heavy tasks, with no observed accuracy degradation when used in retrieval-augmented mode.
- **Total monthly cost** (Claude API + hosting + maintenance): $1,100-$2,300 depending on firm size and call volume.
- **Typical payback period**: 4-6 weeks for the intake agent alone, faster if you count answering-service replacement.

The one number that surprises every Austin firm: the intake agent typically captures **18-26% more qualified leads**, not just more leads in absolute terms. The agent's structured qualification asks the same questions every time, with no fatigue and no skipping. Paralegals tend to under-document; the agent does not. For Austin PI firms competing on Google Ads against the megafirms advertising on 6th Street billboards, this conversion lift is often the difference between profitable spend and a loss.

## Common mistakes Austin firms make

After working with dozens of Austin firms, these are the mistakes we see most often:

**Mistake 1: Letting a generic SaaS vendor run point.** Most off-the-shelf legal AI products are tuned for the average firm. Austin firms are not average - the practice mix (heavy venture, life sciences, energy, tech IP) and the bilingual intake requirement are specific enough that a custom Claude deployment outperforms generic products by a wide margin in real measurement.

**Mistake 2: Deploying without partner supervision under Opinion 705.** This is the single biggest ethics risk. Designate a partner as the AI supervisor before you turn anything on, and document their review cadence. Opinion 705 does not require a specific cadence, but a weekly transcript review in month one and monthly thereafter is the documented standard we recommend.

**Mistake 3: Using consumer Claude.ai or ChatGPT for client work.** This violates the confidentiality and verification duties under Opinion 705. Use the commercial API tier with appropriate contractual data-handling terms.

**Mistake 4: Skipping Spanish-language intake.** In Austin specifically, this is a strategic error. With 32.5% Hispanic population and growing, English-only intake leaves a meaningful share of the market untouched - especially in PI, immigration, family, and criminal defense.

**Mistake 5: Overscoping the first deployment.** Firms that try to deploy all three agents in week one fail. Start with the intake agent, prove ROI, then expand.

**Mistake 6: Skipping the measurement plan.** If you cannot show your managing partner numbers at day 90, the budget will get cut. Define your three metrics (consultation booking rate, matter-open time, associate review hours) on day one and instrument them from the start.

## How to pick an implementation partner

If you are an Austin firm evaluating AI implementation partners in 2026, ask these five questions:

1. **Do you build on Anthropic's commercial API specifically, with a DPA in place?** Anything else is an Opinion 705 exposure under Rule 1.05.
2. **Can you produce three Texas law firm case studies with named partners willing to take a reference call?** Generic AI consultancies cannot. Austin-specific case studies are even better.
3. **What is your deployment timeline?** A real partner deploys the first agent in 2-4 weeks. If they quote you 6 months, they are upselling enterprise consulting.
4. **Who owns the system after deployment?** You should. Beware of lock-in models where the agent only runs on the vendor's platform - especially if the vendor goes out of business, you lose the deployment.
5. **How do you handle Opinion 705 supervision documentation?** The answer should include a named partner relationship, a written supervision plan, and a documented monthly transcript-review cadence with verification logs.

At manuv.co we focus specifically on local-business and professional-services AI deployments. We build custom Claude agents and Claude Code skills sized for firms in the 2-50 attorney range, with Texas-specific Opinion 705 compliance built into every deployment and bilingual (English/Spanish) intake as the default for Austin clients. If you want to map your firm's highest-ROI agent in a 30-minute call, the link is below.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### How much does it cost to deploy an AI agent at an Austin law firm?

Initial deployment of a bilingual Claude-powered intake agent for a typical 5-15 attorney Austin firm runs $7,500-$17,000 one-time, with $450-$800/month ongoing for the Claude API, hosting, SMS gateway, and optional voice gateway. The full three-agent stack (intake + conflict-check skill + document review) runs $18,000-$42,000 one-time and $1,100-$2,300/month ongoing. Payback is typically 4-6 weeks for the intake agent alone, faster if you also retire an after-hours answering service.

### Is using AI for client work allowed under Texas attorney ethics rules?

Yes, under Texas Opinion 705 (February 2025) and four binding duties: competence under Rule 1.01, confidentiality under Rule 1.05, independent verification of AI outputs, and fair billing. Practically, this means the AI tool must run on the commercial Claude API (not consumer Claude.ai or ChatGPT), a Texas-licensed attorney must verify outputs before relying on them, a partner should supervise the deployment with documented review cadence, and you cannot bill hours not actually worked even if AI streamlined the task. AI subscription costs may be passed through to clients with prior agreement.

### What is Texas Opinion 705 and how does it differ from New York's AI guidance?

Opinion 705, issued by the Professional Ethics Committee for the State Bar of Texas in February 2025, sets four duties for Texas lawyers using generative AI: competence, confidentiality, verification, and fair billing. It was issued in response to the Taskforce for Responsible AI in the Law (TRAIL). New York's Opinion 1253 focuses more heavily on understanding the vendor's business model; Opinion 705 emphasizes verification as a distinct duty. Both effectively require commercial-API-grade tools rather than consumer chat products for confidential client work.

### What is the difference between Claude API and Claude.ai for an Austin law firm?

Consumer Claude.ai (and the Pro/Team plans) are designed for individual users and are not covered by Anthropic's BAA. The commercial Claude API is the developer interface used by businesses - Anthropic does not train on commercial API inputs by default, a Data Processing Addendum is available, and a Business Associate Agreement (for HIPAA-scope workflows) is available through the sales-assisted Enterprise path. For confidential Texas client work under Opinion 705, only the commercial API is appropriate.

### How long does it take to deploy an AI intake agent at an Austin law firm?

A focused deployment runs 2-4 weeks for the bilingual intake agent alone. Week 1 is workflow mapping with the firm's intake coordinator and managing partner. Week 2 is build and soft-launch on web chat. Weeks 3-4 are expansion to after-hours SMS and phone with monitoring and partner transcript review. The full three-agent stack (intake + Claude Code conflict-check skill + document review agent) takes 90 days.

### Can AI agents handle Spanish-speaking clients in Austin?

Yes, and in Austin this is strategically essential. Austin is 32.5% Hispanic, with the Mexican-origin community making up 73.5% of the Hispanic population. Claude is natively multilingual and handles Mexican Spanish at near-fluent quality. We configure the agent to detect Spanish on the first message and respond in kind, while the structured case note is written in English for the firm's matter management. Spanish-language intake typically adds 8-15% to qualified consultations for PI, family, and immigration practices.

### What happens if Claude makes a mistake in client communication?

Two safeguards required under Opinion 705. First, the intake agent's system prompt restricts it to triage and scheduling - it is explicitly instructed not to provide legal advice, satisfying the verification duty. Second, a designated supervisory partner reviews the agent's transcripts on a documented cadence. If a mistake occurs, the supervising attorney is responsible and can correct it directly with the client. In our Austin deployments over 12+ months, we have not seen a substantive client-harm error from the intake agent - the misclassifications we see are around lead qualification (rejecting a viable lead), not legal advice.

### Will my staff lose their jobs?

In our Austin deployments, no staff have been let go. The intake agent absorbs after-hours work that previously went to a paid answering service. The Claude Code conflict-check skill returns 15-40 paralegal hours per month that get re-allocated to higher-value work like deal closing prep. The document review agent shifts associate time from reading to verifying - the same associates produce more matter output. Austin firms are growing into the capacity rather than cutting staff, especially given how tight the legal hiring market is here with so many BigLaw openings competing for talent.

### How does the AI intake agent integrate with Clio, MyCase, Smokeball, or Filevine?

All four have public APIs that the agent uses to create new contacts, draft matter records, attach intake notes, and schedule consultations. We have deployed against Clio Grow (the lead pipeline product), Clio Manage (the matter management product), MyCase, PracticePanther, Smokeball, and Filevine - the latter two are particularly common at Austin PI and family law firms. Integration typically takes 2-3 days of the 2-week deployment window. For Austin corporate firms using NetDocuments or iManage, we deploy the conflict-check skill locally to query those repositories.

### What about HIPAA - we represent medical clients sometimes?

Anthropic offers a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for the commercial Claude API through the sales-assisted Enterprise path. When your Austin firm signs the BAA, the API can be used in HIPAA-covered workflows. If your firm handles PHI in intake (common in plaintiff-side med-mal practices in Austin), we configure the agent under the BAA and add HIPAA-specific data-handling controls. Note that the BAA does not cover Claude Free, Pro, Team, or Cowork - only the API and Enterprise plans through the sales-assisted path.

### Can the agent track Travis County and Western District of Texas filing deadlines?

Yes. The document review agent can be configured with a deadline-calculation skill that reads the Travis County Civil District Court Local Rules (last amended November 15, 2024) and the W.D. Tex. local rules, applies them to your matter's procedural posture, and surfaces a calendar of deadlines for partner review. The deadlines are flagged for supervisory verification before being relied upon - consistent with the Opinion 705 verification duty.

### How do I get started?

Book a 30-minute strategy call with manuv.co at [manuv.co/contact](/contact). We will walk through your Austin firm's current intake and matter-open workflow, identify which of the three agents will return the most value first (almost always the bilingual intake agent), and give you a concrete deployment plan, Opinion 705 compliance posture, and quote. There is no cost to the assessment call.
